data.title: AbbVie: "Strong Buy" On Rinvoq Expansions And Immunology Pipeline Addition
data.description:
The last time I wrote about AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV) was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "AbbVie: Skyrizi And Rinvoq Latest FDA Approvals Bode Well For Humira Patent Loss". With this respect to this article, I noted that Humira was approaching the loss of patents and that both Skyrizi and Rinvoq [upadacitinib] would pick up the mantle and carry sales for the company going forward. This is exactly what happened as both of these drugs have been doing quite well in terms of revenues. That is, sales of Skyrizi and Rinvoq sales increased by 62.2% to $4.423 billion and 41.8% to $2.028 billion, respectively. Speaking of Rinvoq, this drug has come a long way and what I want to provide as updates below for this JAK inhibitor candidate is that it was able to achieve positive results from a second pivotal phase 3 study, known as Study 1.

This is part of the entire phase 3 UP-AA program that had both of these two replicative studies to evaluate the use of Rinvoq for the treatment of adolescent and adult patients with severe alopecia areata [AA]. The primary endpoint of this study was met in a statistically significant manner with both 15 mg and 30 mg doses of this drug targeting this group of patients. The beauty of this is that AbbVie intends to submit this data to regulators across the globe in order to notch another FDA approval for it. The truth is that the company continues to expand its approval of this oral JAK inhibitor. For instance, another update that happened in the most recent second-quarter of 2025 was achieving the first ever FDA approval Rinvoq for the treatment of adults with giant cell arteritis [GCA]. While it is expanding this particular drug towards other indications, another update I want to go over is the continued building of its immunology pipeline.

While Skyrizi and Rinvoq are strong, it is already planning for the future with a clinical candidate it obtained through the acquisition of Capstan Therapeutics, known as CPTX2309. This clinical candidate provides a differentiated mode of action of going after B-cell mediated autoimmune disorders. It is a targeted lipid nanoparticle [tLNP] that delivers an mRNA encoding an anti-CD19 chimeric antigen receptor [CAR] capable of generating CD19-specific and CD8+ in vivo CAR-T cells that are capable of depleting B-cells. Most importantly, the ability to treat such patients without any need of prior lymphodepleting chemotherapy agents. With Rinvoq expansions happening recently, plus the acquisition of a potential first-in-class tLNP candidate capable of resetting the immune system in patients with B-cell mediated autoimmune disorders, I believe that AbbVie should remain as a "Strong Buy" rating.

Two Positive Developments For Rinvoq Bode Well For Long-Term Revenue Growth

As I noted above, AbbVie was able to achieve two key positive developments as part of its expansion of its oral JAK inhibitor candidate Rinvoq. The first of such expansions that happened in the second quarter of 2025 was that it was able to achieve a "first" in terms of FDA approval. That is, this drug became the first JAK inhibitor to be approved by the FDA for the treatment of patients with giant cell arteritis [GCA]. This disorder is classified as inflammation that occurs on the lining of arteries on the temporal lobe of a patient. What makes this disorder devastating is that there is no actual known cause of it. Besides causing some symptoms like headaches and jaw pain, this condition gets worse if not treated promptly. How so? Well, if not treated then the inflamed arteries can cause the person to lose their vision in one or both of their eyes. GCA is classified as an autoimmune disorder and as such it occurs because the immune system attacks the blood vessels of the patient. Rinvoq helps these patients by specifically blocking key inflammatory pathways inside of immune cells. Some actions of importance would be the blocking of inflammatory cytokines and activation of JAKs. In essence, the goal is to block the inflammatory response that is activated and ends up damaging the blood vessel walls causing inflammation. The reason for the drug being approved was that the primary endpoint of the phase 3 SELECT-GCA trial, sustained remission at week 52 was met in a statistically significant manner. Rinvoq was compared to placebo on the basis of this primary endpoint. The thing is that the trial recruited patients into each group as follows:

  • 209 patients who received 15 mg of Rinvoq
  • 107 patients who received 7.5 mg of Rinvoq
  • 112 patients who received placebo

The final outcome was that the higher dose of 15 mg of Rinvoq achieved this primary endpoint, in that 46.4% of patients had sustained remission through week 52 compared to 29% of patients on placebo [statistically significant p-value, p=0.002]. The beauty of this approval is that it marks the ninth one for Rinvoq.

The second positive update to make note of would be that the company has a shot at being able to expand the use of Rinvoq towards another indication soon enough. How so? Well, that's because in August of 2025 it announced positive results from Study 1 as part of its phase 3 UP-AA clinical trial program using Rinvoq to treat adult and adolescent patients with severe alopecia areata [AA]. Previous to this, it reported a positive outcome as well for this program, but in Study 2. In this particular trial portion as part of this program it noted that 44.6% and 54.3% of patients, which took 15 mg and 30 mg of Rinvoq had 80% or more scalp hair coverage at week 24 , defined by SALT score ≤ 20. With both studies being equivalent, I believe it is important to go over the trial data from the more recent one in detail, which was Study 1. The other trial, Study 1 also had patients given Rinvoq that ended up seeing 80% or higher scalp hair coverage at Week 24 compared to placebo. The efficacy outcome percentage of patients that reached this goal over a 24-week period compared to placebo are noted below:

  • Patients that took 15 mg of Rinvoq : 45.2% achieved this primary endpoint
  • Patients that took 30 mg of Rinvoq : 55% achieved this primary endpoint
  • Placebo comparator : 1.5%

Should this drug be approved to treat these patients, it will be a welcomed change compared to current treatment options, which are plagued with uncertainty for starters. Plus, the mode of action of Rinvoq is ideal, as in the data release it was noted that hair regrowth was observed both in terms of scalp and non-scalp measures. Another factor to consider is that when patients entered into this study for screening, they had an average baseline SALT score of 84, which translates to 16% of scalp hair coverage. To have a good chunk of patients with hair regrowth and be offered a new treatment option such as this oral JAK inhibitor could be ideal for them. The next step gets even better for AbbVie because it will immediately submit this data from both of these pivotal studies to regulators. This means that the company could achieve another indication under its belt for Rinvoq if it is ultimately approved by the FDA to treat adolescents and adults with severe AA.

Acquisition Of Capstan Brings Forth Candidate To Potentially Reset Immune system in Autoimmune Disorders

Another positive development I want to go over involves the fact that AbbVie spent $2.1 billion to acquire Capstan Therapeutics. The reason for doing so was so that it could obtain potential first-in-class targeted lipid nanoparticle [tLNP] anti-CD-19 CAR-T therapy to target patients with B-cell mediated disorders. With this acquisition, it obtains the ability to advance the use of CPTX2309 to help with targeting these patients. What makes this candidate, plus the technology of Capstan Therapeutics so highly ideal, is that there is in vivo programming of the CAR. This is a very good thing because of the programming being done in this fashion, there is no need for prior lymphodepletion before providing patients with CPTX2309. The way that this candidate works is by delivering a messenger RNA [mRNA] payload of an anti-CD-19 CAR to cytotoxic CD8+ T cells and thus programming takes place in an in vivo fashion. These reprogrammed CD8+ T cells now have the component to deplete B-cells, which have the CD-19 protein on their cell's surface.

That is, this is a hallmark component of B-cell mediated autoimmune disorders. The bottom line is that this offers a new scope of CAR T targeting, whereby the new modality of in vivo engineering of cells provides an excellent way to have off-the-shelf capability. This brings me to the final point, which is that the company not only obtains CPTX2309 as a candidate, but also gains the use of Capstan's tLNP platform technology. The point is that any RNA payload can likely be used to engineer cells in an in vivo fashion. Besides being a novel mode of action, it provides the ability to do what I stated beforehand, which is to reset the immune system. Furthermore, achieving this generates a scope far beyond only targeting the symptoms of autoimmune disorders, but also targeting the underlying cause of them. In essence, CPTX2309 and the tLNP platform technology can have a mandate to beyond what may have been achieved with candidates in immunology like Rinvoq and Skyrizi so far. Not only that, but it could change the treatment paradigm moving forward as some CAR-T therapies are modified ex vivo [outside of the body]. Being able to achieve programming in vivo could provide far more advantages than just off-the-shelf capability alone. To that end, the acquisition of Capstan Therapeutics was completed on August 19, 2025.

Financials

AbbVie is doing quite well with respect to earnings and it continues to see growth in revenues, which is a good thing. For instance, in Q2 of 2052 it reported global net revenues of $15.42 billion, which is an increase of 6.6% year over year. The truth is that the company has well established growth beyond what I went over above with respect to its immunology portfolio. Where it saw the best overall growth in a year-over-year fashion was instead with its neuroscience portfolio. In other words, this would be the targeting of neurological disorders. Consider that the neuroscience portfolio grew by 24.2% to $2.683 billion. Drugs in this specific category are: Vraylar, Botox, Qulipta, and Ubrelvy. The clear winner though, believe it or not, was Botox from here which saw revenue growth to $928 million.

This ended up being a year-over-year growth of 14.1%. The immunology portfolio is still growing at 9% year over year, but a problem that remains is a decline in sales for Humira which continues to decrease and in the most recent Q2 of 2025, dropped in sales by 58.1% to $1.180 billion. If not for this, the category would be higher. Still, it goes back to what I have shown above, that this immunology portfolio can continue to grow thanks to new indication approvals for Rinvoq for starters. What would also end up helping out is if tLNP CPTX2309 is successfully advanced through all clinical testing and ultimately improved. I believe it would be huge because of the ability to provide off-the-shelf CAR-Ts and ones that won't require lymphodepletion for the patient in question.

AbbVie is expected to report Q3 of 2025 financial results on Friday October 31, 2025 before the market opens. The expectation is that Q3 earnings per share [EPS] is expected to achieve year-over-year growth, but full overall year-adjusted EPS guidance has been lowered. What's the reason for this? It has been lowered to a range to be between $10.38 - $10.58 and it is because of a host of intangible assets due to a merger or transaction. The key expectation and what is important to watch for would be the neuroscience portfolio as I described above, which saw revenue grow by 24.2%. Total year-over-year revenue growth is expected to be higher, forecasted by analysts to be around 6.6% during the quarter.

AbbVie's valuation in terms of price-to-earnings [P/E] ratio is abnormally high at a trailing 12-month basis of 103.26, compared to its own peers of 21.37x and the biotech industry average P/E in the United States of 15.77x. However, I want to note that the expectation for this company is not based on what its current P/E ratio is set at. In essence, it is largely based on the promise of its future growth drivers that it has in place. With the forward P/E of AbbVie being 16.84 as the basis for future growth. The expansion of its immunology portfolio is one promising aspect, where there is an expectation for expansion based on moving towards other indications. In addition, there is the acquisition of Capstan Therapeutics, which brings forth CPTX2309 as a targeted lipid nanoparticle [tLNP] and anti-CD19 CAR-T therapy candidate to target patients with B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders. This is one promise of expanding its immunology portfolio. There is room for future growth here, as the immunology portfolio at the moment is growing with revenues at 9.5% year over year. However, the best expectation of its future growth driver would be the best-growing portfolio it has, which is the neuroscience one. How so? Well, this specific portfolio saw revenue growth by 24.2% year-over-year to $2.683 billion. The point here is that even though the P/E ratio is high, investors are investing based on the expectation that the company has a lot of growth drivers in place. Thus, while the P/E is higher than it once was historically, there is still room for it to grow its earnings based on future prospects. I believe that the valuation is worth is based on its strategy of seeing growth in several different portfolios. Of course, being 22 higher in P/E than its peers means a huge valuation risk if the expectations of its ongoing trials and expected expansions are not met.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in AbbVie. The first risk to consider is the immunology portfolio with respect to Rinvoq and more broadly. The first risk to consider would be the latest approval of this drug for the treatment of patients with GCA. The reason why is because even with this approval there is no guarantee that it will cause revenues for Rinvoq to pick up because of it. Secondly, the goal is to get the AA indication as well for this drug. I believe that, if approved, this could actually change the scope of revenues to be made with this drug. Why is that? Consider that the global Alopecia areata market is expected to reach $16.02 billion by 2030. The risk here is that despite the primary endpoints being met in both Study 1 and Study 2, there is no assurance that the FDA is ultimately going to grant marketing approval of Rinvoq for this specific indication.

The second risk to consider would be declining sales of Humira. As I indicated in my prior article, the company was set to meet a major problem with declining sales for this drug. This continues to be the case and in the most recent Q2 of 2025 earnings, is recognized with a decline in sales by 58.1% to $1.180 billion. The good news is that AbbVie has Skyrizi and Rinvoq in place to counter the lost revenues with Humira. Going forward, I think such deals like acquiring companies to add as part of its immunology portfolio may end up paying off.

Speaking of which, this brings forth the third and final risk to consider here, which would be the latest acquisition of Capstan Therapeutics. The reason why is because the in vivo targeted lipid nanoparticle [tLNP] anti-CD-19 therapy is currently being explored in a phase 1 study targeting patients with B-cell mediated autoimmune disorders. The risk here is that despite the belief that in vivo programming of CD8 expressing cytotoxic T cells will allow for the depletion of B-cells in an effective and durable manner without lymphodepletion, this may not end up being the case. There is no guarantee that the results to be released from the phase 1 study are going to be positive for starters. Nor that the data to be produced from this early-stage study is going to be enough for AbbVie to want to continue to advance this program for phase 2 clinical testing.

Conclusion

AbbVie has been able to achieve some expansions this year in terms of its drug Rinvoq. The first of which, noted above, would be the FDA approval of this oral JAK inhibitor for the treatment of patients with GCA. Beyond the scope of this regulatory approval, I believe that the stronger indication would be the completion of the two late-stage studies as part of the phase 3 AA-UP program, with the targeting of patients with AA. As I have shown above, Rinvoq was able to allow 44.6% and 54.3% of patients, who took 15 mg and 30 mg of Rinvoq, to have 80% or more scalp hair coverage at week 24. This is compared to the low 1.5% of patients that attained this efficacy outcome measure who were given placebo instead.

The bottom line here is that I believe it is still bullish that AbbVie is looking to continue growth in its immunology portfolio. Especially since it is still reeling from the loss of its Humira patents. The addition of tLNP CPTX2309 could end up changing how CAR T therapies work in treating patients with B-cell mediated autoimmune disorders. That is, to alter the CAR T landscape with the scope of in vivo programming instead of ex vivo [outside of the body] modification. Lastly, it is not relying on its immunology portfolio for growth at all. As I have shown above in the financials section, it was able to see double-digit percentage growth of 24.2% to $2.683 billion in Q2 of 2025. Based on everything I have stated, I believe that AbbVie's stock should be kept with a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: 3 Price Catalysts For Eli Lilly
data.description:
When I last wrote about the obesity treatments' specialist Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) in August, there was a 50% upside to the stock following its crash after disappointing drug trial results. In the two months since, the stock has realised ~30% of the upside far exceeding the gains for sector, as seen from the 9% gains for the Health Care Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLV).

Clearly, there are still some 20% gains to be made, with no updates on the earnings outlook since. But there are also catalysts for the stock underway, which are likely to support a price uptick for LLY, but can also pose risks. Here are three of them.

#1. Agreement with the US government

The threat of 100% tariffs on imported pharmaceutical products has been a key challenge looming over the sector this year. But with Pfizer's (PFE) deal with the US government at the end of September, and Anglo-Swedish cancer treatments' provider AstraZeneca's (AZN) agreement last week, the risk has reduced substantially.

Pfizer is expected to invest some $70 billion in the US, in lieu of which, it gets a grace period of three years on tariffs. Its understanding with the government also entails lowering drug prices by 50% for patients in the US. AstraZeneca came to a similar agreement.

Since these agreements, LLY has jumped by ~14%. With Eli Lilly speculated to make a deal soon as well, the stock could rise even further, especially as it has already decided to invest $50 billion in the US in the foreseeable future as well. With this investment amount, it's likely to get a relaxation on tariffs as well. As and when the agreement comes through, the stock can rally even more.

#2. Earnings outlook at potential risk

While broadly speaking, an agreement with the US government will be positive for LLY, it does risk reducing margins for Eli Lilly. After all, Pfizer's deal entails selling medicines to US based patients at par with prices in other developed countries, which translates into reducing them.

A similar expectation is expected to be placed on Eli Lilly. In fact, a Seeking Alpha report has said that Ozempic's price would be reduced by the government, which is a close enough indicator. Also this wouldn't be the first time that weight loss treatments' companies are coming under the government lens. Even last year, the Biden administration was critical of the price charged by both Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk (NVO).

Here's the twist in the tale, though. Despite reducing prices, the company's operating margin has only risen since. From an already high 39.1% in Q2 2024, the number had increased to 45.8% by Q2 2025. Whether it's able to pull off the same again remains to be seen. We could get an indication as early as October 30, when the company releases its Q3 2025 results. If the outlook is indeed reduced, the price upside will get diminished and could negatively impact the price.

#3. Trial results please investors

Next, at the time I last checked on the stock in August, results for the company's trial GLP-1 treatment, Orforglipron, had made investors unhappy. The stock saw a sharp 14% plunge on the day they were released since they weren't at par with Eli Lilly's highly successful current weight loss treatment, Zepbound.

However, even at that time, clear positives for Orforglipron were evident. First, there was the ease of administering it as a pill instead of an injectable. Second, it had a positive impact on other biomarkers. And third, it had the potential to be produced at scale (see segment "Mounjaro, Zepbound, and the case for Orforglipron" in the link for details).

The case for Orforglipron has only strengthened since, based on its impact for a narrower segment of overweight trial participants, who also have diabetes. In late August, the company said that such participants saw a 12.4% reduction in weight with a dosage of 36 mg daily after 72 weeks. The news resulted in ~6% jump in stock price on the day.

Subsequently, the treatment's diabetes related biomarkers also showed superior results compared with other oral competitors like oral semaglutide, which is marketed by Eli Novo Nordisk as Rybelsus. The biomarker A1C saw a 2.1% drop with Orforglipron, compared with a 1.4% decline with oral semaglutide at 52 weeks.

Earlier this week, the company also said that Orforglipron also showed better performance compared to dapagliflozin, which is sold by AstraZeneca as Fraxiga. At 40 weeks, it lowered A1C by 1.7% compared with dapagliflozin's 0.8%.

With the recent buildup around the treatment coupled with its potential to impact LLY's price, it can be yet another catalyst for the stock when another study on its outcomes becomes available in January 2026 (see slide below).

Looking beyond weight loss and diabetes

Outside of the key price catalysts, the company's recent advances in areas of treatment other than cardio-metabolic health also merit a mention. Specifically, progress cancer treatments are particularly notable here considering that Verzenio has already brought in some 9.4% of the revenues in H1 2025.

Its treatment for advanced or metastatic breast cancer, branded as Inluriyo, received an approval by the US FDA as it showed better outcomes than alternative therapies. This followed the FDA's approval for Verzenio for late stage breast cancer, which can improve survival in combination with other therapies. The company also reported positive trial results for its leukemia treatment Jaypirca last month.

They might not move the price for LLY immediately, but they are certainly worth keeping a lookout for.

What next?

In a nutshell, investors can look out for three potential price catalysts for LLY going forward. The first, which can happen anytime now, is an agreement with the Trump administration on tariffs, which can be positive.

Second, the Q3 2025 results due at the end of October, which can go either way. If they factor in any price reductions following an agreement with the government, the earnings outlook can be negatively impacted. Third, further trial results for Orforglipron can impact LLY's price, after it has moved the stock notably more than once since August.

On the whole, there's still more to look forward to with respect to Eli Lilly than not. And for that reason, I'm maintaining a Strong Buy rating on it for now. However, it could be reduced if there are negative developments regarding any of the three catalysts.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Truist: Attractive Given Solid Capital And Reinvestment Benefits (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Truist Financial (NYSE:TFC) have been a mixed performer over the past year, losing about 4% of their value. Investors received some welcome news Friday when the company reported solid earnings, sending the stock up 4%. The regional banking sector has come under immense pressure recently as concerns have mounted around potential private credit losses, though TFC earnings should assuage some fears. I last covered Truist in July when I downgraded shares to a “Hold,” and since then they have pulled back 3%. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Truist.

In the company’s third quarter, Truist Financial earned $1.04 per share, beating estimates by $0.05 as revenue rose by 2% to $5.2 billion. While this quarter was not incredibly strong, it was a solid result across most metrics. Importantly, it is enabling investors to breathe a sigh of relief that the bank is not seeing problems in its corporate-centric loan portfolio despite problems bubbling up at other institutions. Additionally, its improving adjusted capital position is enabling more aggressive capital returns.

Balance sheet growth is still modest

Looking first at its balance sheet, deposits declined by $3 billion sequentially to $397 billion. Despite its favorable geographic location in the Southeast U.S., Truist’s deposit performance has been a source of disappointment for me. TFC has been making progress, though, and deposits are up over 3% from last year. It is a bit disappointing to see deposit costs still running 5bps above Q1 and only 24bps lower than last year. I like to see banks pass on 35-45% of Fed rate cuts, and TFC has been closer to 25%. Its decision to move more slowly on rates has likely contributed to better deposit growth performance, but this has been a reason margins have faced some pressure.

Average loans rose by 2.5% from Q2 to $320 billion. Loans are up almost 6% from last year, as Truist has been able to accelerate loan growth this year. While tariffs paused business decision-making for a bit during Q2, that headwind seems to have passed with lending reaccelerating over the past 4-5 months.

Relative to most regional banks, Truist’s loan book is tilted more towards business lending—that accounts for half of loans. Just 6% of loans are to commercial real estate (“CRE”), one of the lowest exposures in the sector. Given the challenges CRE has faced, this has been a meaningful tailwind, insulating it from credit pressure. With increased fear around private credit, its corporate lending exposure may garner more scrutiny, but thus far, quality is solid (more on credit quality below).

Given these factors, net interest income was $3.7 billion, up just 1% from last year. Truist’s net interest margin (“NIM”) compressed 1bp to 3.01%, also down 11bps from a year ago. I do expect we are near or at the bottom in NIM, even with Fed rate cuts, thanks to fixed reinvestment.

It still has $119 billion of fixed-rate securities, which have an average yield of just 2.89%. As these continue to mature ($3 billion mature in Q4), TFC can reinvest proceeds in today’s higher rate environment. Given how low its portfolio yield is, even if we see a handful of additional Fed rate cuts, fixed reinvestment should be a persistent tailwind to NIM and NII through 2027. It also has $11 billion of fixed rate loans maturing next quarter.

Separately, noninterest income rose by $80 million from a year ago to $1.56 billion—up over 10% sequentially. Wealth management hit a new record, up 7% to $374 million thanks to fund flows and rising markets. Given elevated markets, I expect a sequential increase here in Q4. Investment banking rebounded after a weak Q2 to $323 million, more in line with last year, as capital markets reopened following strains in Q2. I see some potential for downward pressure in Q4.

Credit quality is still solid

Turning to credit quality, there are no signs of problems or of worsening corporate borrowing. Truist had 48bps of net charge-off in the quarter, better than the 55bps a year ago. In fact, Truist’s $385 million of charge-offs were the lowest in over a year. We did see nonperforming loans tick back up to 48bps, which is consistent with where they have been most of the past year, apart from a seemingly transitory dip in Q2.

TFC took $436 million in provisions for credit losses, down $52 million from last quarter, and allowances cover 1.54% of loans, down 6bps from last year and flat sequentially. Even with fairly stable NPLs, Truist has built about $150 million in reserves over the past year, providing some protection against potential weakening in the economy. Reserves now provide over 3x coverage of NPLs, which I view as very strong (I am comfortable with levels above 2.5x). With excess reserve coverage, Truist’s balance sheet is already prepared for a potential rise in NPLs, should the economy weaken or private credit worsen, without needing to set aside greater provisions.

Capital is solid, enabling shareholder returns as ROE improves

Truist has a strong balance sheet with an 11% tier 1 common equity (CET1) ratio as of Q2. This was down 60bps from a year ago. One reason why Truist is running with such strong capital is that it has a $6.4 billion loss in AOCI, largely due to unrealized losses on its $114 billion securities portfolio from buying bonds when interest rates were lower. Adjusting for this, its capital position would be closer to 9.6%. I like to see a bank of TFC’s size have at least 9.5% of capital and closer to 10%, so it is now within range. As fixed income securities continue to mature and pull to par, the AOCI headwind should get steadily smaller.

Alongside earnings, management maintained guidance for 1.5-2.5% revenue growth. It expects to accelerate buybacks to $750 million in Q4 from $500 million in Q3. Longer term, Truist is targeting a 15% return on equity by 2027 from 13.6% last quarter, aided by fixed asset reinvestment and accelerating share repurchases. It aims for a 10.0% CET1 ratio by the end of 2027. Essentially, TFC has been maintaining excess published CET1, given its AOCI loss, but as this loss diminishes, it can bring CET1 down, which should mechanistically support enhanced returns. Combined with fixed investment tailwinds, I view this path to ROE expansion as highly credible, outside a recession.

Back in July, I was targeting $3.85-$4.00 in 2025 EPS, and I see TFC coming in at the high end of the range this year, likely $3.92-$4.00. Given at least $2 billion in buybacks, I see TFC as poised to deliver $4.20-$4.45 in EPS next year. That leaves shares at about 10x 2026 earnings, which I view as attractive given its solid capital position and secure 5% dividend yield. With shares down since my last coverage but credit results better than feared, I am moving shares back up to a “Buy,” maintaining my 11x forward multiple for a ~$47.50 price target, given solid capital but limited deposit growth. With a 15+% total return opportunity, I would use the pullback to add TFC.

data.title: Moderna: More Downside In The Stock But The Cancer Vaccine Offers Potential
data.description:
Moderna's (NASDAQ:MRNA) stock has continued to fall in 2025, despite a new vaccine approval and additional clinical readouts ahead. In January, I rated MRNA a sell, expecting the company would continue burning cash with little to show for it in the near-term. We're now closer to potential readouts from the company's cancer vaccine trials where a strong result could help turn things around for the company. This article looks at the phase 3 trial of MRNA's personalized cancer vaccine in melanoma patients and potential insights from the competitor's cancer vaccine.

The cancer vaccine: mRNA-4157

The first trial of mRNA-4157 likely to read out in 2026 is the INTerpath-001 study in melanoma patients who have undergone resection of their tumor. That study tests adjuvant treatment with mRNA-4157 in combination with pembrolizumab vs pembrolizumab plus placebo, and has a recurrence-free survival (RFS) endpoint. The time of analysis will depend on the rate those recurrences occur, but the company continues to guide towards a 2026 readout.

... So we actually will need to accrue a certain number of cases before we are able to look at those data. We have gone, of course, as we look at that case accrual and modeled it based on our Phase II study as well as other KEYTRUDA studies. And so we are cautiously optimistic that we'll be able to share data from this study in 2026...

Rose Loughlin, EVP of Research at MRNA, Bernstein Insights 2nd Annual Healthcare Forum, September 25, 2025.

Competitor BioNTech (BNTX) also has a its own individualized neoantigen therapy, autogene cevumeran (BNT122), that also vaccinates the patient against their own cancer neoantigens. BNTX in concert with Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) ran the Imcode-001 trial comparing BNT122 plus pembrolizumab to pembrolizumab in 130 patients with previously untreated, advanced melanoma. Unfortunately, rather than provide strong support for the idea, that vaccinating patients against many of their own cancer neoantigens can boost the response to pembrolizumab in melanoma, I find the results from Imcode-001 to be a little disappointing. Imcode-001 missed its primary endpoint; progression-free survival (PFS) was approximately eight months in both arms. Further, a supposed numerical benefit on overall survival wasn't statistically significant and we don't have much more detail than that currently (see final results document, page 5).

Even though other endpoints included objective response rate and duration of response, no detail was provided by BNTX on that front either and RHHBY has discontinued development of autogene cevumeran in that indication. Of course the Imcode-001 study is in stage IV or stage IIIC/IIID unresectable patients who have measurable disease, and MRNA's phase 2 work with mRNA-4157 (KEYNOTE-942) is in the adjuvant setting with patients who have undergone successful surgical resection (this can still include stage IV patients, but they have to have achieved successful surgical resection of their disease). As such, while the disappointing results from Imcode-001 don't necessarily provide support for the idea that an mRNA cancer vaccine targeting 10's of neoantigens can work in cancer, it also might not disprove the idea that such a therapeutic can work. It may just be a case of needing to choose the right setting.

Notably, BNTX/RHHBY's autogene cevumeran isn't discontinued entirely. There is still ongoing work, including in the adjuvant setting. One such trial, BNT122-01 uses adjuvant treatment with autogene cevumeran in colorectal cancer, and has a control arm of watchful waiting. The trial is still recruiting according to clinicaltrials.gov, although a timeline of late-2025 to early-2026 had previously been discussed.

If BNTX does produce something positive from BNT122-01 in late 2025, or perhaps early 2026, it could provide additional support for the idea that an mRNA cancer vaccine can succeed in certain settings. On the one hand, the readthrough to mRNA-4157 could be positive, or it could confirm competition for MRNA from BNTX.

Financial Overview

MRNA reported total revenue of $142M in Q2'25, with $114M of that coming from Spikevax sales ($88M US, $26M international). The company expects H2'25 revenues to be higher (in line with the winter/fall COVID season), and reported a 2025 revenue range of $1.5B-$2.2B, the upper end of that guidance down from $2.5B with Q1'25 earnings. For Q2'25, MRNA also reported; costs of sales of $119M, R&D expenses of $700M, SG&A of $230M and a net loss of $825M or -$2.13 per share. The company finished the quarter with $7.5B in cash, cash equivalents and investments and is currently guiding for approximately $6B at year end.

MRNA currently trades with a $10.6B market cap ($27.14 per share).

Risks

There are several risks to a short position in MRNA, although this article is not advocating for that, some risks will be discussed here.

Firstly, MRNA could report stronger than expected results with Q3'25 earnings, which might increase confidence the name can reach or exceed its 2025 revenue guidance, triggering a rally in the stock.

Secondly, MRNA could report positive results from its phase 3 study of its cytomegalovirus vaccine, with the study fully enrolled and the company expecting a final analysis in 2025.

Lastly, BNTX could announce positive results from its trials with autogene cevumeran, such as from the adjuvant trial in colorectal cancer. It is hard to interpret the reaction MRNA's stock might face to that, but it could be viewed as confirming the potential of these modern individualized mRNA cancer vaccines (mRNA-4157 and autogene cevumeran). Positive results for BNTX could thus trigger a rally for MRNA too.

Conclusion and rating

MRNA's previous phase 2 data with mRNA-4157 in KEYNOTE-942 study has been widely discussed for the promise the vaccine showed in reducing recurrences. There is nothing in science like replication however, and given decades of fairly poor results with cancer vaccines the market will certainly want to see that here, to know the KEYNOTE-942 results were no fluke. A 2026 readout from MRNA's phase 3 melanoma trial of mRNA-4157 represents an opportunity to change the fate of MRNA as a company and the fate of the stock. Nonetheless, in the mean time, the recent approval of a next-gen COVID vaccine mNEXSPIKE plus continued middling results with its marketed vaccines (Spikevax and mResvia) haven't done anything to stop MRNA's stock performing poorly. I don't see any near-term change to that as likely, and so investors might be stuck waiting for readouts from MRNA's other programs, like the trials of its cancer vaccine.

I currently rate MRNA a sell, but I'm not really advocating for a short position, I just wouldn't hold a name that doesn't seem to be participating in the current biotech bull market.

data.title: SABA: Cheap Alpha From A Skin-In-The-Game CEF Activist
data.description:
In this article we highlight the Saba Capital Income & Opportunities Fund II (NYSE:SABA). In our view SABA offers a significant diversification for the average CEF portfolio, piggy backs on the manager's successful activism in the space and offers structure advantages such as its unusually shareholder-friendly fee structure.

SABA was previously the Templeton Global Income Fund (GIM) which the manager took over at the start of 2024. SABA trades at a 9.2% discount and a 7.7% current yield.

Fund Snapshot

SABA has a fairly unusual allocation, holding a number of private funds, SPACs, crypto exposure and other assets that retail investors wouldn't normally get exposure to. The equity sleeve is very well diversified with little Mag-7 holdings that many investors are likely already overweight.

SABA has a distribution of $0.058 which has been unchanged since it took control of the fund. Despite its modest yield, the fund does distribute quite a bit of ROC. The fiscal year-to-date picture which covers 10 months is as follows. This does make it a borderline income asset however this is mitigated by a number of other positives which are discussed below.

Apart from its ability to provide less common exposure and diversification in income portfolios, one reason why we like SABA is its shareholder-friendly fee structure.

The fund's management fee is somewhat unusual both for the level of the fee as well as the fact that it is applied on net assets. The fee level works out to slightly below 0.70% given the fund's fee schedule. As of the last shareholder report, net assets were around $266m.

Fees in the CEF space tend to range around 0.75%-1.25%, paid on total assets. The impact of paying fees on total assets versus net assets is roughly an increase in 50%. In other words, for a typical leveraged CEF with a fee of 1% on total assets, the overall fee on net assets is 1.5%.

The net result is that both the low fee level and applying the fee on net, rather than total, assets means that SABA's fee is about half that of the typical leveraged CEF. This adds around 0.75% of tailwind to the fund's performance per annum.

The fund's fee structure is also much lower than that of its sister fund Saba Capital Income & Opportunities Fund (BRW). For example, total expenses for SABA worked out to an 0.95% on total assets annualized for the last semi-annual period. The same figure for BRW was 2.3%.

Unlike for the vast majority of the CEF market where the management fee is paid on total assets, a few paid on net assets does not incentivize management to push leverage up to high levels. A high level of leverage does not only add risk for shareholders, it also generates, what we call, inefficient income given the cost of leverage eats into the income generated by leveraged assets.

For instance, a typical credit facility used by CEFs will charge something akin to 3-month term SOFR + 1%. So if a fund holds assets with a weighted-average credit spread (i.e. yield over and above "risk-free" assets like Treasuries) of 4%, that means leverage eats up a quarter of the additional spread even before we apply the management fee.

The second reason we like SABA is the manager's "skin in the game" element of the fund. Saba owns a bit more than 10% of SABA. This shows that the manager's own fortunes are tightly linked to the success of the fund itself. How the fund performs is more important for Saba than the fees that it collects by managing it.

Saba is the most active and, arguably successful, CEF activist investor. Perhaps the cleanest way to understand Saba's skill in CEF allocation and activism is by looking at the performance of its ETF (CEFS).

These are impressive figures that can go head to head with just about any other CEF, particularly on a leveraged-adjusted basis (CEFS is not leveraged). Having a manager allocate its own assets to one of its funds is very uncommon, however there is no better way to align interests between shareholders and the manager.

The third reason we like SABA is that it can short assets which is also fairly unusual as the vast majority of CEFs are long only. This gives it an additional lever to add value over and above the rest of the CEF space.

For the time being we like SABA over its sister fund BRW despite BRW briefly trading at a wider discount recently.

This is for two reasons. One, BRW is currently undergoing a rights offering which overstates its NAV. The NAV dilution looks to be around 3%, meaning that the discount of BRW is actually quite a bit tighter than that of SABA. And two, because SABA has a significantly lower fee, it should trade at a tighter discount than BRW as there is no other structural difference between the funds.

Since Saba took over the fund, it has performed well in the context of other Credit / Multi-sector / Hybrid CEFs. Funds that have outperformed it have tended to be funds with significant convertible bond exposure (which have performed well due to the broader Tech rally) or funds with a lot of equity exposure, well in excess of SABA.

The fund continues to trade at an attractive discount that remains wider of the Hybrid CEF sector and more than double of the median CEF sector.

All in all, we view SABA as an attractive diversifier in income portfolios. It has a very shareholder-friendly fee structure which contributes to performance. It benefits from Saba CEF activism and it trades at an attractive discount.

data.title: The Market's Liquidity Source Is Running Dry
data.description:
Liquidity conditions in the market have deteriorated. Reserve balances for the week of October 16 fell to $2.988 trillion. The effects of the Treasury General Account refill, first discussed in July, are now being felt across various parts of the market.

Signs of Market Strain

The most obvious place the tightening of liquidity is being felt is in the overnight funding market, and that has resulted in the Secured Overnight Financing Rate, to surge to 4.3%. That rate is now actually above the lower bound of the Fed Funds Target rate, before the Fed cut rates by 25 bps in mid-September.

At this point, SOFR is trading 15 bps above the interest on reserve balances, which is an extremely wide spread, and one that has been historically seen previously in October and December 2024. But more importantly, is that this spread has been steadily widening and trending higher.

Perhaps more importantly, the Fed's effective funds rate is actually moving higher, climbing from 4.08% following the 25 bps rate cut in mid-September to 4.11% as of October 16. It may not sound like a lot, but you can count on one hand how many times the Fed Funds Rate has changed since August 2023, besides a rate cut, which has been three times. Those times have happened since mid-September; otherwise, the rate hadn't budged.

Additionally, another sign that liquidity is tight is that the Fed's standing repo facility is now being used a bit more actively. On October 15 and 16, the SRF was used for $6.75 billion and $8.35 billion, respectively. This facility is used when institutions are short on cash and need liquidity; the Fed provides liquidity in exchange for collateral.

Treasury Settlement Days

The tight conditions may only worsen as we move through the rest of the year, provided the Treasury continues to issue large sums of debt. In fact, both October 15 and 16, when the SRF was used, were also Treasury settlement dates. In fact, the spike in SRF usage has recently occurred on Treasury settlement dates. These are days when liquidity is drained from the overnight funding market.

The next two dates for a potential spike in usage for SRF activity will come on October 21, when there is a Treasury settlement of $29.0 billion, and October 23, when there is a settlement for $23.0 billion.

Given that the Treasury isn't likely to stop issuing new debt any time soon, this trend we are seeing in liquidity conditions and overnight funding rates isn't likely to get better anytime soon; in fact, it is likely to get worse, as more and more cash gets sucked out of the market.

It could set up what is likely to be a pretty rough end to 2025, because in the end, risk assets rely on liquidity and leverage, and when the source of liquidity and leverage dries up, risk assets will have a difficult time holding up.

data.title: AEye: Nvidia And Blue-Band Change The Game
data.description:
Latest announcements:

AEye, Inc. (NASDAQ:LIDR), back in late August, announced a partnership with Blue-Band LLC, a pioneer in smart mobility and intelligent traffic technologies. The partnership brings together AEye’s advanced Apollo lidar system with Blue-Band’s Integrator-AI platform under the newly launched OPTIS program.

According to Matt Fisch, CEO of AEye, in the press release:

“OPTIS™ was built to turn perception into action…By integrating Blue-Band’s AI platform, we’re extending OPTIS’™ capability to interface directly with traffic control systems, providing real time analytics to enable lifesaving and congestion-reducing actions at traffic intersections.”

Kevin Yorke, CEO of Blue-Band, said that

“Partnering with AEye brings the power of long-range lidar to our platform, enhancing our ability to provide intelligent, scalable solutions for traffic management and urban mobility.”

I think the announcement highlights how AEye is shifting from being just a lidar hardware supplier to becoming a critical enabler in smart infrastructure. The OPTIS initiative effectively bridges perception and decision-making, not only detecting vehicles, pedestrians, and hazards, but also triggering real-time responses that can optimize traffic flow and improve safety. If AEye can convert this collaboration into pilot deployments with cities, it could mark a turning point toward commercialization and recurring revenue visibility, something the stock has lacked.

This collaboration also goes hand in hand with AEye’s earlier partnership with Nvidia (NVDA), announced after its Apollo LiDAR system was integrated into Nvidia’s DRIVE AGX platform, another major validation and commercial opportunity. The Nvidia partnership, as I previously discussed in my initiation article, puts AEye on the map in autonomous driving, opening doors to deals with automakers and transforming its long-term growth prospects, making AEye’s sensor fully compatible and certified to work seamlessly within Nvidia’s autonomous ecosystem.

Investment thesis:

Taken together, the Blue-Band collaboration and Nvidia partnership show that AEye is strategically broadening its reach, from traffic intelligence to autonomous driving, connecting two of the most promising growth frontiers in AI-powered mobility.

Still, both these are priced into the stock.

The stock is up around 11% over the past month, and it’s pulling back to the $2.70 range in pre-market trading on Friday, down over 6% and building on the 8% pullback on Thursday.

As part of a broader market sell-off, AEye is also feeling the weight of profit-taking across small-cap tech and speculative AI names. After a strong multi-week run fueled by the Blue-Band and Nvidia announcements, the current pullback looks more like a cooling phase rather than a change in fundamentals, in my opinion. I remain buy-rated.

Earnings:

The company reported 2Q25 earnings in early August. They expect revenue growth to stay moderate for the remainder of the year, while management focuses on expanding customer partnerships and introducing its technology into new applications. Full-year 2025 cash burn is now projected to land at the upper end of the previously guided $27–$29 million range. According to CFO Tierney on the earnings call:

This updated outlook reflects several key factors: the positive impact of a recent lease settlement, if we continue paying down the convertible note in cash, one time professional service fees and anticipated investments in product development to support upcoming customer programs. While these are near-term costs, they are aligned with our broader strategy to scale efficiently and position the company for long-term growth.

Management also confirmed that recent capital raises have strengthened AEye’s financial position, extending its cash runway into 2027, as the company

“ended the quarter with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $19.2 million. Since quarter end, we've more than tripled this balance, extending our cash runway into 2027.”

On the financial front, GAAP operating expenses for the quarter came in at $8.6 million, up from $6.8 million in 1Q25. Non-GAAP operating expenses rose to $6.8 million, an increase of $1.2 million sequentially. The company also reported a GAAP net loss of $9.3 million, or $0.48 per share, compared with $8 million, or $0.46 per share, sequentially. On a non-GAAP basis, the loss came to $6.7 million, or $0.35 per share, versus $5.5 million, or $0.31 per share, previously.

Valuation and What's Next

AEye is still in the early stages of scaling, so traditional profitability metrics like P/E or EV/EBITDA don’t capture much about its current valuation. Instead, it makes more sense to assess the company through revenue-based multiples. The stock trades at an EV/Sales (TTM) of 419.42 and a forward EV/Sales of 492.45, versus sector medians of 3.71 and 3.56, respectively. Its Price/Sales (TTM) multiple is 160.09, and the forward Price/Sales rises sharply to 574.31, far above the sector’s at 3.5x. From a balance sheet standpoint, the Price/Book ratio stands at 5.83, compared to a sector median of 3.90.

These stretched multiples underscore how AEye remains a pre-revenue, high-conviction bet on future adoption rather than a company valued on current earnings power.

Going forward, I’ll be watching for more partnerships and early-stage deployments that can validate the company’s technology in real-world settings. The recent tie-ups with Blue-Band and Nvidia have established strong proof points, but the next step for AEye will be converting these collaborations into commercial contracts and recurring revenue streams.

data.title: 4 Signs The Market Is Overdue For A Correction
data.description:
Despite continued worries about trade tensions, a potential AI bubble forming, sticky inflation, and a faltering job market, the major indexes continue to trade near all-time highs. Equities overall are trading at valuation levels not seen since the last innings of the Internet Boom, and by some measures at a more extreme valuation.

In today's column, I highlight four signs pointing to the markets having once again become overdue for a correction.

1. Margin Debt Is Exploding

One great measure that 'greed' has been fully embraced by market participants is retail margin debt. And individual investors have boosted their margin debt since April of this year by a whopping 39%. Margin debt now stands at a record $1.13 trillion. And leverage can make selloffs much more brutal as margin calls sweep over the market.

One just has to look at what happened to cryptocurrency investors last Friday, when markets had their worst one-day selloff since the first half of April. Bitcoin fell approximately 10% in a day, and Friday was the largest crypto liquidation event in history. As you can see in the chart above, huge increases in margin debt have come before huge market crashes this century.

2. Insiders Are Selling

While the dumb money is increasing their margin debt, the smart money or insiders are increasingly on the sidelines and taking chips off the table. The insider buy/sell ratio is right near the lowest levels since this metric started to be tracked. Jeff Bezos' stake in Amazon (AMZN) just dipped below 10% for the first time, it should be noted. Historically, when insider purchases surge higher, they have indicated great times to buy equities. Obviously, this reading is signaling just the opposite currently.

3. Cracks Developing In The Credit Markets

Some notable cracks have started to develop in the credit markets over the past month. Subprime auto lender Tricolor Holdings filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in September with some $2 billion in obligations. Tricolor was once the 7th leading independent used car dealership in the country. Tricolor's bankruptcy left borrowers, banks, and investors facing heavy financial losses. JP Morgan Chase (JPM) took a $170 million charge due to its exposure to Tricolor in its third-quarter results that came out this week.

Soon after Tricolor's march into bankruptcy, auto parts supplier First Brands went into bankruptcy as well, but with obligations exceeding $10 billion. It should be noted that Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB), which announced a merger with Comerica Incorporated (CMA) this month, is on the hook with First Brands for some $700 million. UBS has just over $500 million of exposure to this debacle. Both bankruptcy events seem to have contained their share of shenanigans.

On Thursday, new loan issues were disclosed by both Zion Bancorporation (ZION) and Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL), knocking both stocks down more than 10% in trading yesterday. It was the worst day for the KBW Regional Banking Index versus the S&P 500 since March 2023, during the Silicon Valley Bank implosion.

Are these the only cockroaches out there, or will more emerge from the woodwork? Given U.S. corporate bankruptcies are running at a 15-year high, I would wager on the latter.

4. The AI Narrative Falters

Most of the gains in the markets over the past three years have been driven by the AI Narrative, which is the largest technology paradigm shift since the birth of the internet in the mid-1990s. NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT) have grown so large, both stocks have market caps larger than the annual GDPs of either France or the U.K. These two names also capture nearly 15 cents of every dollar passively being invested in S&P Index funds. A self-reinforcing and virtuous cycle. The opposite would be true, obviously, if fund flows reversed.

However, as I noted in a recent article, there are chinks developing in this narrative that have driven the markets higher. More and more analysts and pundits are starting to question this AI vendor-financing circle.

It is reminiscent of the vendor financing surge from the likes of Cisco Systems (CSCO), Lucent, and Global Crossing that became a big driver of the latter innings of the Internet Boom at the turn of the century.

McKinsey recently projected the global AI data center buildout will require a cumulative $7 trillion in investment by 2030. Will revenues generated by AI ever justify the investments and debt the tech giants are applying to this effort?

I have my doubts. Amazon Web Services (IE, the 'Cloud') was birthed in 2006. This year, this business is delivering revenues just over $120 billion on an annual run rate. Will AI revenues greatly exceed this and ramp up much faster?

Finally, we have the massive amounts of electricity and freshwater needed to operate these huge data centers. Will the electrical grid be able to bring the additional capacity online fast enough for this buildout? Will more and more communities like Tucson rise up and reject these projects because of these demands? How big of a hit will stocks take if/when this AI Narrative falters further? Only the shadow knows as my late father liked to quip.

Given this view of the overall market, my portfolio remains cautiously positioned. Most of my holdings are within covered call positions around the few equities I am finding in this market with reasonable valuations. I offered up three of these stocks earlier this week. The rest of my portfolio is in short-term Treasuries and cash. I will start to deploy some of this dry powder into the market incrementally on the next significant dip -- an event that, for the reasons listed above, seems likely on the near-term horizon.

data.title: Nvidia's Venture Portfolio Is Its Secret Weapon
data.description:
The semiconductor industry is interesting because it is simultaneously one of the most profitable sectors in the world, while also being one of the most cyclical.

Strong boom periods are often followed by periods of contraction, which leads to a lot of volatility within the space. If you’ve invested in semiconductors through a broad-based fund like iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), you may not have noticed this because different parts of the semiconductor value chain have complementing cycles. Semiconductor manufacturing equipment has different lead times and is exposed to different Capex than memory chips, for example.

That said, make no mistake - historically, semiconductor companies have a boom/bust profile, which is one of the core risks with a stock like Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA).

Sure, cloud titans and scrappy upstarts are buying truckloads of GPUs to power next generation AI applications, but what happens when buyers feel like they have enough chips?

As time has gone on, it appears as though the unique nature of AI and the winner-take-most economics mean that we expect to see a strong base level of demand for Nvidia’s cutting edge chips, especially as the company releases new products.

However, the specter of cyclicality remains.

Normally, this would be a significant long-term risk for investors, but the company has taken a path - it has chosen to reinvest windfall profits into the AI venture ecosystem - to the tune of tens of billions of dollars. Not only that, but the company actively supports its investments with preferred access to chips, demand for compute and more.

Effectively, Nvidia is trading it’s cutting edge chips for equity stakes in application layer companies, a strategy that could produce long-term cash flow stability.

Not only that, but given the breadth and depth of Nvidia's venture portfolio, we expect at least a handful of these companies will produce unbelievable, call-option style returns the materially boost the firm's market cap.

While many analysts spend time focused on the AI buildout, in this article we wanted to focus on Nvidia’s venture holdings. We'll dive into the company's top stakes, outline the strategic importance of these investments, and explain why the portfolio acts as a strong bullish driver for the stock.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Before going to deep on the venture portfolio, it’s probably worth discussing Nvidia’s core business, given that this is - currently - the real money maker. We’ve covered this in depth previously, but suffice it to say that the company is firing on all cylinders right now.

Over the last 12 months, the firm produced $165 billion in revenue, and kept $86.6 billion of that as net income. This level of net income makes Nvidia the world’s fifth most profitable company, only behind Google (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT) Apple (AAPL) and Saudi Aramco:

Not only that, but Nvidia enjoys much higher margins then similarly sized players, with 58% operating margins and 52% net margins:

Finally, on the growth front, over the last 12 months Nvidia grew revenues by 71% YoY, lapping the growth of other leading companies - many times over.

Driving the success, as you’re likely aware, is the AI boom, which has been funded by cloud titans like the aforementioned Google and Microsoft, along with other key cloud players like Amazon (AMZN) and Oracle (ORCL).

On the application side, firms like OpenAI have also committed significant capital towards compute in order to drive product improvements. All of this is a virtuous cycle for Nvidia. Currently producing the best-in-class Blackwell chip, the firm is in a strong position to capture almost all of this demand.

But what happens when firms decide they don’t need to keep up the breakneck pace? What happens when incremental value for building new data centers drops, as it eventually will?

As value generation transfers from the base layer chip producers to application layer companies, one would expect chipmakers, including Nvidia, AMD (AMD), and Intel (INTC) to experience reduced margins and a period of industry contraction.

The Portfolio

This is where the Nvidia's venture portfolio comes in.

Over the last few years NVDA has been pouring most of its profits into investments in the AI startup ecosystem. As you can see, a high percentage of the company’s retained earnings have been kept as short and long term investments:

Initially, these investments benefited Nvidia as much as they benefited underlying startups, due to the fact that many of these firms turned around and purchased NVDA GPUs with the money.

Some have criticized this strategy as a circular investment loop, and we understand why some have likened this to the dot-com bubble.

However, a few specific deals aside, we believe that Nvidia has successfully grown revenues while trading chips for equity stakes in businesses in a healthy way. This is an incredibly strategic way to allocate capital from picks and shovels position.

Now, these equity stakes represent significant upside potential for Nvidia going forward.

Here are 3 investments we think are particularly interesting.

First up, we have OpenAI. Arguably in the lead of the AI race with its popular ChatGPT and Sora products, OpenAI's valuation has grown to $500 billion as of its most recent funding round. While Nvidia's $100 billion investment into the company is expected to execute over a number of years, the size of this investment will still likely leave Nvidia with a significant, double digit stake in the company.

Given how quickly OpenAI has grown revenues, it’s possible that this investment will buttress Nvidia's consolidated cash flow. This - of course - is due to OpenAI's high-quality subscription model that keeps cash coming in the door.

In addition to the stake in OpenAI, Nvidia will also own part of the company's investments, which includes a 10% stake in AMD that came as a part of a recent chip deal. NVDA gains solid diversification and cash flow here from a single allocation.

Secondly, Nvidia’s largest public stock holding is CoreWeave (CRWV), an AI cloud company akin to if Amazon Web Services were a standalone firm built strictly to power AI applications.

It’s true that CRWV is significantly smaller than cloud juggernauts like Microsoft, Google and Amazon, but Nvidia has been supporting the company with preferred access to Blackwell chips, which in turn has made its offering much more competitive within a marketplace facing compute shortages.

Not only that, but Nvidia has also built out a compute marketplace called Lepton, where CRWV is a top provider. By doing this, NVDA is essentially supporting the company by funneling demand for compute directly into the ecosystem.

With a contribution view, in Q2 of 2025, AWS produced roughly $10.1 billion in operating income, with an operating margin of roughly 30%, showcasing what a profitable end state CRWV could potentially end up in with over the next 5 to 10 years.

We’re neutral on CoreWeave as a stock right now given ongoing valuation concerns, but over the long-term, we expect Nvidia's stake in the company to bolster and stabilize consolidated cash flow.

Finally, to give some color to the 'call option' side of Nvidia’s investments, recently, the company closed a $500 million investment into AI driving startup Wayve.

This investment builds on Nvidia's participation in Wayve's series C funding round, and should give the company considerable working capital to bring its driving technology to market.

While, there is considerable competition already in the autonomous driving space by big companies, including Tesla (TSLA) and Waymo (which we’ve covered before) it appears as though Wayve is focused on using Nvidia's technology to power it’s Gen 3 platform, which may end up panning out as a success.

We would argue that the payout diagram for Wayve is quite risky, but the upside is also tremendous if the firm manages to capture part of the autonomous driving market.

Aside from these three investments, NVDA has also made considerable investments into other companies across the ecosystem, including public stakes in firms like Recursion Pharmaceuticals (RXRX), Nebius (NBIS) (which we're quite bullish on) and Intel (INTC), alongside private stakes in everything from DataBricks - a mature venture backed data company - to xAI, Elon Musk's OpenAI competitor:

Other notable investments include £2 billion dedicated to the UK's AI ecosystem, a $500 million+ investment into Perplexity AI, and a $600 million investment into Quantinium, a quantum computing startup.

The Portfolio is truly a "who's who" in the world of AI. That way, no matter who profits the most from AI, NVDA appears set to benefit.

Taken together, Nvidia's organic business and investment portfolio present considerable, de-risked upside, especially as value creation moves from the chip layer to platform companies.

Valuation

Then, we come to the valuation. As enticing NVDA's core business and investment portfolio are, if the valuation didn’t make any sense, then we’d be forced onto the sidelines for the time being.

Thankfully, we think NVDA's current valuation and long-term investment profile appear reasonable.

At present, Nvidia is currently trading in a blended price/earnings ratio of roughly 44x, which is above similarly sized peers like Microsoft, Apple and Google.

However, Nvidia also enjoys a much higher historical growth rate than these companies, and analysts expect the firm to grow at roughly 21% per year over the next 5 to 6 years:

Of course, with the growth rate expected to slow down, we’d expect the market to reevaluate the company to a lower multiple, potentially towards the 30x range.

Well, this does present a multiple contraction scenario, the broader investment case here is still strong even at 30x. In between now and the end of the decade, growth rates imply, a 12% annualized return:

Given how expensive the S&P 500 (SP500) looks right now, we expect this will top the market average.

However, another important piece to consider is the company's investment portfolio, which could grow substantially in size and value over the next five years, especially as the AI ecosystem matures.

Some stakes that NVDA has poured hundreds of millions or billions into could be worth multiples of that by the end of the decade, driving additional upside for shareholders on top of the core business.

This could push annualized returns closer to 15 or 20%, especially as OpenAI - in particular - continues to grow.

This informs our bullish rating.

Risks

Of course, there are risks associated with an investment in Nvidia, including the aforementioned Capex risk, along with catch-up concerns by competitors, including AMD and Intel.

On the GPU cycle frame, it appears as though the company has been considerably de-risked as a result of the broader venture portfolio.

Similarly, competitive risks would normally represent a much more acute threat, but between Nvidia's backdoor stake in AMD through OpenAI, and the company's direct investment into Intel, we’d argue that as the sector grows, so too should Nvidia.

The only risk that we see that Nvidia has not accounted for is broader market valuation risk. Both Nvidia and the S&P 500 both are trading at frothy valuations, and while Nvidia’s multiple is not necessarily unearned, we expect that the stock could be sold off at the first sign of macroeconomic trouble.

You’ll often see this happen - when the market corrects, expensive winners are typically trimmed before "value" laggards are.

Thus, there’s definitely some timing risk associated with an investment in Nvidia. However, we don’t see this as a material long-term threat to the company's organic performance.

Summary

Here’s the bottom line. Right now, some investors may think that Nvidia looks expensive, trading at 44x earnings with considerable Capex cycle risk and competitive threats from companies like AMD and Intel.

However, aside from Nvidia’s core business lines, which continue to pump out profits, the firm's growing venture portfolio should stabilize Nvidia’s long-term value, while providing call-option-like upside on top of what analysts currently expect.

As a result, we remain bullish on shares of Nvidia, even as the stock trades near all-time highs.

Cheers~

data.title: NIKE Isn't Out Of The Woods Yet
data.description:
NIKE, Inc. (NYSE:NKE) remains one of the world's premier brands, particularly in sports, but its stock has completely lost its luster in the past few years. As shown in the chart below, NIKE has seen 52-week losses of about 14%, while the broader market has set record high after record high. It is understandable that they are in a tough position, though. There's fierce competition, weak consumer confidence, and tariff headwinds to deal with. They reported the most recent earnings at the end of September, and today we'll take a look to see if there are signs of a turnaround for the business.

In the below analysis, it is shown that there are signs that an inflection point is in. However, gross margin contraction continues, and weak cash flows have pressured capital returns. In addition, guidance for Q2 is rather soft, making the near-term outlook still undesirable. Despite continued headwinds and risks, the valuation is currently quite elevated, and so I would say NIKE does not present an attractive risk/reward at this point. Therefore, I have decided to initiate a sell rating.

Performance Has Been Pressured

We'll get into their FY2026 Q1 performance in just a second, but before that, I just want to briefly take a look at the company's performance over the past few years. As you can see, in FY2025, NIKE has underperformed in each of these key metrics. Top and bottom line declines are worrying, and the ROIC has weakened considerably. With that in mind, the major gap between the S&P 500's performance and NIKE stock's performance is really quite a rational reaction in my view. Let's now look for some signs of stabilization.

Top Line Declines Pause

As stated earlier, NIKE reported their most recent earnings at the end of September, so just over two weeks ago. For the top line, they generated a total of $11.73 billion in revenues. That represents a growth rate of 1.01%. If you heard this growth rate in isolation, it wouldn't be impressive. But considering that revenues have been declining every quarter since FY2024 Q4, this marks a major shift for the better. Just in the previous quarter, revenues were down 12% YoY, and so it has been a rather remarkable turn of events. This potentially shows that demand is starting to stabilize as the tariff shock to the U.S. consumer may have subsided somewhat. Lastly, NIKE crushed analyst expectations as they beat them by a whopping $730.23 million, and so their top-line results were much better than feared.

Margin Remains Weak

While the positive revenue growth rate is definitely a welcome sign, there are still considerable concerns for their business. In their breakdown of revenues, they reported that NIKE Direct revenues were down 4% YoY to $4.5 billion. These are their sales through their website and their own retail stores. On the other hand, wholesale revenues increased by 7% to $6.8 billion. The problem with this is that Direct sales have higher margins since there is no middleman. The company would prefer to sell through their own channels rather than rely on other retailers. For gross margin, NIKE reported a 320 basis point contraction to 42.2%. This was the result of higher discounts needed for sales and also because of the channel mix. Therefore, despite some top-line improvement, a closer look shows that the business may not be out of the woods yet.

Capital Returns At Risk?

With the massive margin contraction, it is no surprise that the bottom line results were unfavorable. They reported a diluted EPS of $0.49, down 30% YoY. Do note that back in Q4, EPS dropped by 86%, and so there is actually an improvement. In the previous quarter, gross margin contracted by an even more worrying 440 basis points. Anyhow, while the EPS decline in Q1 wasn't what investors wanted to see, cash flow may be even more of a concern.

As you can see above, NIKE generated a total of $222 million in operating cash flows. That is a 44% decline from the year-ago quarter. While this is already potentially worrying, the company increased CAPEX by 72.5% YoY to $207 million. As a result, free cash flows are barely positive and are at their worst level since FY2024 Q1. This potentially makes capital returns to shareholders a bit shaky.

Seemingly as a result of cash flow weakness, NIKE cut their overall capital returns significantly versus the prior year period. They actually increased their total dividend payout by 6% to $591 million, but for share repurchases, there was a major decrease. In Q1, they bought back a total of $126 million in stock, according to Seeking Alpha data, down 89% YoY. That is a stunning change in policy and shows that the cash flow weakness could be making management cautious. As you can see above, Seeking Alpha Quant also seems a bit wary about their dividend safety, as it is only rated as a D-. At this point, I would say that a dividend cut is highly unlikely, as they still have $8.6 billion in cash and equivalents and short-term investments. Therefore, even if FCF weakness persists, NIKE still has room to continue paying out their dividend.

Q2 Guidance

For FY2026 Q2, NIKE is said to be expecting a YoY revenue decline in the low single digits. That is a bit of a disappointment considering that they saw positive growth in their Q1. This clearly shows that while the worst may be over, demand is still on very shaky ground at the moment.

As for gross margin, they are expecting a 300 to 375 basis point contraction after including an expected new 175 basis point tariff headwind. At the midpoint, this contraction will be bigger than in Q1, and so the unfavorable channel mix is likely to continue for the company.

Lastly, NIKE expects that SG&A expense will increase in the high single-digit percentage range. With revenues expected to decline, this increase in expenses signals that corporate efficiency isn't going to be particularly encouraging either. As a whole, I would say this outlook shows that major speed bumps remain on their path to recovery.

Valuation Is Questionable

NIKE has seen a major TTM P/E ratio expansion after a multiyear low in the multiple earlier this year. As you can see, during the middle of 2025, the P/E broke above its 3 year median, and the current reading of 35.16 represents quite a high level relative to the past few years. At this point, there are reasons to believe that this valuation may not be appropriate.

Despite some signs that the company has seen an inflection point, they are far from being a strong performer. Gross margin continues to contract, and the company has had to cut back on share repurchases, which appears to be a sign of low confidence in cash flows. Furthermore, the guidance discussed above is rather soft and indicates that headwinds could persist.

For sure, the TTM P/E ratio has expanded due to a steep drop in earnings, and that potentially makes the valuation seem higher than it is. However, as you can see, NIKE's forward P/E is also rather elevated, and so investors may be a bit too optimistic regarding the prospects of a turnaround in the near future. Lastly, note that Seeking Alpha Quant rates their overall valuation a D- as a sign of considerable overvaluation.

Final Thoughts

As stated at the beginning, NIKE is a world-class brand. In the very long run, I do see the company returning to stronger performance, but now is not a great time to buy in to the stock, in my view. As of now, there is still much uncertainty over whether the turnaround is actually happening. Sure, there are improvements for the top line, but many metrics are still in unnerving positions. If the stock's valuation was cheap, that would help offset these risks. However, as discussed above, NIKE's quite expensive both according to the TTM P/E and the forward P/E. Therefore, even for investors that believe in the long term outlook for the company, there will likely be better buying opportunities down the road. For that reason, the stock is currently a sell in my view.
